question.text: Inaugural question: The repeal of the English Corn Laws in 1846 was the first major victory bringing this principle into practice. Ulysses S. Grant predicted that “within 200 years, when America has gotten out of ___ all that it can offer, it too will adopt” this principle. Karl Marx said tongue-in-cheek that “those who wish for it desire it in order to alleviate the condition of the working class.” America adopted the principle about 100 years after Grant’s prediction, not 200 years. Name this economic principle that is facing heavy resistance in the 2020s.

discussion.text: the blank is “protectionism”, the opposite of “free trade”

Architect Paul Revere Williams was born in LA in 1894. This LA legend helped build and shape modern LA as we know it today, with his work spanning nearly six decades. Williams was a pioneer in a field with very few architects of color. To deal with conditions in the times when Williams was an architect, he developed a special skill to work on projects in ‘different neighborhoods’ with a ‘different set of people'. What skill did he develop, which is somewhat unique even today?

answer.confirmation_text: because he couldn’t stand on the same side as his colleagues

discussion.text: He was the first to do scale models?
I think i’ve read about this - he would draw ulta?
he had to draw upside down
Because ppl didnt like sitting next to a black person
Because people would sit opposite him ‎
because he couldn’t stand on the same side as his colleagues
Yess! Many of his white clients felt uncomfortable sitting next to a black man.

question.text: One meaning for the name of this place is 'a word that ends in a vowel.' It is located in a ravine formed by the Waghora river in Central India. question.text: Next question: king _ takes his daughter _ to __. They return _ years later and _ marries _. Second and fifth blanks are the same. Bonus points if you can identify what is this an example of.

answer.text: King Kakudmi takes his daughter Revathi to Brahma Loka. They return 116 million years later and Revathi marries Balarama. This is one of the earliest examples of time dilation in ancient texts (from the Bhagavata Purana).

question.text: The 1996 India vs England test match at Lord's is remembered for two unforgettable debuts for India with Sourav Ganguly and Rahul Dravid taking on the English attack. However this match also served as the last international test match with this long serving Englishman calling it a day. Known for his wit, eagle eye and accurate decisions who is this legend of the game?

discussion.text: Correct. Didn't know that this was his last game 😅

question.text: Alan Freed was a disc jockey at radio station WJW in 1951. As he played uptempo Black rhythm and blues records on his show, he started referring to them with a three-word phrase. This became the name of a genre that came to define American music for the rest of the century. What term did Freed coin?

answer.confirmation_text: rock and roll is the correct answer

discussion.text: I have two guesses for the two parts of question 😂 First part - Jazz and Blues. Last line - Rock and roll
rock and roll is the correct answer

question.text: Collectively, these flags denote a certain entity and were used to represent it (e.g. at olympics etc). Which entity? Brownie points if you can ID each flag. [image: two British India flags]

answer.text: British India. First flag: British Indian Ensign; second flag: Governor General's Standard (indicated by the Crown).

discussion.text: that's the star of India, the flag of British Raj
Yes. Both are British India flags. Can anyone ID the specific flags?
ok then the first one is the flag of the Raj, second one is the Viceroy's flag?
The first is the British Indian Ensign. The bottom is the Governor General's Standard, as indicated by the Crown.

question.text: ID this flag. [image: dark blue flag with Indian national emblem] As a general rule all flags in this series are either historical or otherwise no longer in use.

answer.text: Erstwhile Presidential Standard of India (now replaced by the tricolor)
answer.confirmation_text: Correct! This is the erstwhile Presidential Standard. Now we just use the tricolor

discussion.text: I am thinking this one is a flag associated with the Supreme Court of India
Flags of executive legislature and judiciary in British India? And Army?
ah, the standard of the rashtrapati?
Correct! This is the erstwhile Presidential Standard. Now we just use the tricolor

question.text: ID this flag. The resemblance to another flag we all know (and I love) is not coincidental as per many theories, and might be a clue. [image: flag with Union Jack in canton and 13 red-white horizontal stripes]

answer.text: Flag of the East India Company (many believe it directly inspired the design of the early US flag)

discussion.text: US flag just before independence? Or just after. Then they replaced it with the stars?
not the current flag of Hawaii, but an earlier version
also the flag of the East India Company

question.text: Which country's (fictional) flag is this? [image]

answer.text: Latveria — the fictional country ruled by Doctor Doom in the Marvel universe

question.text: ID this flag/coat of arms. The building, and the name of the flag shown here are literally the answer — the place denoted herein still exists in some form. [image: coat of arms with a fort, St. George slaying dragon, beach, trees]

answer.text: Madras Presidency (the building is Fort St. George, the flag uses St. George's Cross; Fort St. George is now the TN government secretariat)

discussion.text: Coconut, paani aur fort par English, can't be goa, pondy or diu.. is it related to Madras?
The building is Ft. St. George, the flag is St. George's cross, and the dragon refers to the story of St. George slaying the dragon

question.text: Guess who, and what was their historical achievement? [image of a person]

answer.text: Govind Vallabh Mavlankar — first Speaker of the elected Lok Sabha (popularly called Dadasaheb Mavlankar)
answer.confirmation_text: Yes. First speaker of the elected lok sabha.

question.text: I recently asked a question regarding the prime meridian on earth. In the case of earth, the location of the prime meridian is arbitrary, and many cities have enjoyed that place of geographical privilege throughout history. However, in the case of the moon, the prime meridian is an easy choice. How is the prime meridian for the moon defined?

answer.text: The meridian facing Earth — the center line of the face of the moon visible from Earth (since the moon is tidally locked, this is a fixed, natural choice)

question.text: Abelmoschus esculentus is a flowering plant in the mallow family, and it is valued for its edible green seed pods. Originally a tropical plant, its geographical origin is disputed; India, East Africa and West Africa are suggested as possibilities with strong indications that India was the likely place of origin. Researchers are pursuing its potential use as a biofuel. It has at least three names that are used in the English language.
